coredata.dc:title: One-step hydrothermal synthesis of F,N,S-doped photoluminescent carbon dots with yellowish-orange emission for sensitive Cu2+ ion detection: Environmental and biomedical applications

coredata.dc:description: Herein, fluorine-, nitrogen-, and sulfur-doped carbon dots (FNS-CDs) were prepared via a one-step hydrothermal synthesis using flufenamic acid and thiourea as precursors, which showed yellowish-orange fluorescence both in aqueous solution and in the solid state. The as-prepared FNS-CDs were monodisperse and had an average diameter of 4 nm, good water solubility, a fluorescence quantum yield of 18.63 %, and excellent pH and ionic strength stability. The optimal excitation/emission wavelengths of the FNS-CDs were 406/570 nm, with an excitation-dependent wavelength range of 365–435 nm. The surface states, morphologies, and elemental compositions of the FNS-CDs were characterized using FTIR, XRD, TEM, and XPS. In addition, the FNS-CDs without any further modification exhibited high selectivity and sensitivity as nanosensors for Cu2+ over other metal ions. The PL quenching phenomenon can be used to detect Cu2+ ions within a linear range of 1–25 μM with a detection limit of 83.10 nM and an association constant of 1.29 × 104 M−1. The PL of the FNS-CDs was significantly quenched by Cu2+ ions through static quenching and was restored upon the subsequent addition of EDTA. The practical application was demonstrated through the detection of Cu2+ ions in real water samples, yielding recovery rates ranging from 97.8 % to 103.9 % with an RSD below 2.5 %. The FNS-CDs/Cu2+ complex exhibits potent antibacterial activity against S. aureus (85.27 %±2.1 %) and E. coli (91.31 %±1.6 %), whereas the FNS-CDs/Cu2+ has a higher inhibitory effect on the growth of E. coli. Furthermore, the FNS-CDs (100 μg/mL) were used for cell imaging, showing low toxicity to HCT-116 cells exhibited yellowish-orange fluorescence under a confocal microscope.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) UV–vis spectrum of as-prepared FNS-CDs (0.5 mg/mL). Inset shows colors of FNS-CD aqueous solution under daylight and UV light irradiation at 365 nm. (b) PL excitation and emission spectrum of FNS-CDs (0.5 mg/mL). (c) PL intensity and (d) normalized PL intensity of FNS-CDs (0.5 mg/mL) at various excitations from 365 to 435 nm.
PL spectral intensity of FNS-CDs (0.5 mg/mL) at different pH values (a), 0–1.0 M NaCl ionic strengths (b), time intervals (c), and storage stabilities (λexc 406 nm).
(a) FTIR spectra, (b) XRD pattern, (c) TEM analysis, and (d) particle size measurements of the FNS-CDs.
(a) XPS survey scan spectra of FNS-CDs and high-resolution XPS spectra of (b) F1s, (c) N1s, (d) S2p, (e) C1s, and (f) O1s.
(a) Photoluminescent spectra, (b) intensity profile, and (c) daylight and UV light photographic images of FNS-CDs (0.5 mg/mL) in presence of various metal ions (25 μM) in pH 7.4 solutions.

(a) Photoluminescent spectra of FNS-CDs (0.5 mg/mL) in presence of Cu2+ ions (1–25 μM) in pH 7.4 solutions. (b) Job’s plot analysis of PL intensity vs. mole fraction of Cu2+ ions. (c) Stern–Volmer plot based on relative PL intensity [P0/P] vs. [Cu2+] concentrations at 570 nm. (d) Measurement of limit of detection based on calibration curve of FNS-CDs in presence of Cu2+ ions at 570 nm.

(a) Photoluminescent spectra, (b) intensity profile, and (c) daylight and UV light photographic images of FNS-CDs (0.5 mg/mL) and FNS-CDs/Cu2+ (Cu2+ ions; 25 μM) in presence of various metal ions (100 μM) in pH 7.4 solutions.

(a) PL spectra of FNS-CDs, FNS-CDs/Cu2+, and FNS-CDs/Cu2+ with EDTA, (b) corresponding solution contrast images of FNS-CDs, FNS-CDs/Cu2+, and FNS-CDs/Cu2+ with EDTA, (c) reversibility cycle from PL spectra of FNS-CDs (0.5 mg/mL) after sequential addition of Cu2+ ions (25 μM) and EDTA (100 μM) in pH 7.4 PBS buffer solutions, and (d) time-dependent PL intensity changes of sensor FNS-CDs (0.5 mg/mL) at 570 nm upon addition of Cu2+ ions (25 μM) in pH 7.4 PBS buffer solutions with excitation at 406 nm.
Antibacterial activities of control, FNS-CDs, and FNS-CDs/Cu2+ complex against E. coli and S. aureus bacteria. (FNS-CDs;100 μg/mL, Cu2+; 25 μM).

(a) WST-assay-based cytotoxicity assay of HCT-116 cells after incubation for 24 h with various concentrations of FNS-CDs (0–100 μg/mL) and (b) bright-field, fluorescent, and merged fluorescence microscopic images of HCT-116 cells after incubation for 24 h with FNS-CDs and FNS-CDs/Cu2+.
Schematic for preparation of yellowish-orange emissive FNS-CDs via one-step hydrothermal method using flufenamic acid and thiourea as precursors with Cu2+ ion detection.
Determination of Cu2+ ions in different water samples using the proposed method.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein, fluorine-, nitrogen-, and sulfur-doped carbon dots (FNS-CDs) were prepared via a one-step hydrothermal synthesis using flufenamic acid and thiourea as precursors, which showed yellowish-orange fluorescence both in aqueous solution and in the solid state. The as-prepared FNS-CDs were monodisperse and had an average diameter of 4 nm, good water solubility, a fluorescence quantum yield of 18.63 %, and excellent pH and ionic strength stability. The optimal excitation/emission wavelengths of the FNS-CDs were 406/570 nm, with an excitation-dependent wavelength range of 365–435 nm. The surface states, morphologies, and elemental compositions of the FNS-CDs were characterized using FTIR, XRD, TEM, and XPS. In addition, the FNS-CDs without any further modification exhibited high selectivity and sensitivity as nanosensors for Cu2+ over other metal ions. The PL quenching phenomenon can be used to detect Cu2+ ions within a linear range of 1–25 μM with a detection limit of 83.10 nM and an association constant of 1.29 × 104 M−1. The PL of the FNS-CDs was significantly quenched by Cu2+ ions through static quenching and was restored upon the subsequent addition of EDTA. The practical application was demonstrated through the detection of Cu2+ ions in real water samples, yielding recovery rates ranging from 97.8 % to 103.9 % with an RSD below 2.5 %. The FNS-CDs/Cu2+ complex exhibits potent antibacterial activity against S. aureus (85.27 %±2.1 %) and E. coli (91.31 %±1.6 %), whereas the FNS-CDs/Cu2+ has a higher inhibitory effect on the growth of E. coli. Furthermore, the FNS-CDs (100 μg/mL) were used for cell imaging, showing low toxicity to HCT-116 cells exhibited yellowish-orange fluorescence under a confocal microscope.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Earth is inhabited by a diverse array of microscopic organisms, including bacteria, fungi, viruses, protozoa, and higher organisms, such as animals and plants [1]. A notable relationship exists between humans and microbes in the human gut, where beneficial bacteria aid in digestion [2]. However, excessive use of antibiotics has resulted in the development of multidrug-resistant pathogens, creating a worldwide health crisis [3]. Bacteria adapt rapidly to antibiotics, making infections difficult to treat and increasing mortality rates. To address this challenge, new antibacterial agents with mechanisms distinct from those of traditional antibiotics are urgently required. Carbon-based nanomaterials are promising candidates in the field of biomedicine because of their small size, biocompatibility, and distinctive physicochemical properties [4]. These materials show considerable potential for various biomedical applications, including drug delivery systems for chemotherapy and other bioactive compounds [5]. Recent studies have explored the potential of carbon nanomaterials, such as carbon fibers, carbon nanotubes, and carbon dots (CDs), in cancer treatment [6–9]. Studies have shown that these materials can enhance apoptosis (programmed cell death) and inhibit cancer cell proliferation through various signaling pathways. This study underscores the growing significance of carbon-based nanomaterials for advancing both antibacterial therapies and cancer treatments.

In recent years, heteroatom-doped CDs have attracted significant attention owing to their unique physicochemical properties and diverse biological applications [10–12]. The incorporation of heteroatoms into the CD structures plays a crucial role in enhancing their functional properties [13–17]. When doped with elements, such as nitrogen, fluorine, boron, sulfur, or phosphorus, these nanoscale carbon materials exhibit enhanced photoluminescence (PL) [18,19], chemical stability, and biocompatibility, rendering them suitable for various biomedical applications [20–22]. Heteroatom doping introduces more active sites and alters the electronic configuration of CDs, leading to improved optical and chemical properties [23]. These modifications are critical for enhancing the biological activity of CDs and making them more effective antibacterial and anticancer agents [24–27]. CDs exhibit superior antibacterial properties owing to several factors. First, their ultrasmall scale allows them to easily traverse bacterial lipid bilayers and interact with intracellular components, effectively disrupting bacterial function [25]. Second, their abundant surface groups possess various charge properties that enable them to bind to bacterial membranes via electrostatic interactions, leading to membrane damage and compromised bacterial integrity [26]. Finally, the CDs demonstrate impressive photodynamic or photothermal effects when excited by light. This enables them to generate reactive oxygen species (ROS) or heat, which induces oxidative stress or thermal damage within bacterial cells and ultimately causes bacterial death [28]. These unique characteristics position CDs as favorable candidates for the development of innovative antibacterial agents to combat antibiotic-resistant bacteria and enhance biomedical treatments. Recently, the potential use of CDs in cancer theranostics has garnered significant attention [29,30]. In the context of cancer cells, CDs exhibit intriguing interactions and activities. Their ability to selectively target cancer cells via surface modifications enabling specific receptor binding has been explored. Moreover, CDs have demonstrated efficacy in imaging cancer cells using fluorescence imaging techniques, providing real-time visualization and diagnosis [31]. The multifaceted capabilities of carbon to target, image, and treat cancer cells underscore their potential as versatile tools for oncological research and clinical applications.

Metals are essential inorganic molecules abundant in the human body and play pivotal roles in various metabolic functions [32]. Transition and heavy metals infiltrate the environment through industrial activities, such as pharmaceutical manufacturing, agricultural pesticide use, and pipe corrosion [33]. Copper (Cu2+), the third most prevalent metal in the body, plays critical roles in oxygen transport, enzyme catalysis, and electron transfer [34]. Imbalances in copper ion concentrations can lead to a spectrum of disorders, including Alzheimer’s disease, prion diseases, familial conditions such as muscular dystrophy and Parkinson’s disease, gastrointestinal issues, and damage to the liver and kidneys [35–37]. Conversely, excessive Cu2+ accumulation poses environmental toxicity risks [38]. Owing to the detrimental impact of heavy metal ions on human health, numerous studies have focused on developing novel analytical methods for detecting and quantifying these ions in real samples, particularly Cu2+ ions. Existing techniques, such as atomic absorption spectroscopy [39], electrochemical analysis [40], and inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry [41], have been traditionally employed; however, they are hindered by time-consuming sample preparation, expensive equipment, and the need for skilled operators. Therefore, inexpensive, rapid, and simple techniques that offer high sensitivity and selectivity for the detection of heavy metal ions are urgently required. To effectively monitor excess Cu2+ ions in human cells and tissues, efficient fluorescence sensors capable of visualization through fluorescence imaging need to be developed [42]. The design of aqueous-mediated Cu2+ sensors that provide precise outputs, specifically for biological applications is crucial [43,44]. These advancements promise to enhance our ability to monitor and mitigate the health and environmental impacts of exposure to heavy metal ions. This study aims to elucidate the mechanisms underlying the biological efficacy of heteroatom-doped CDs with Cu2+ ion complexes through a detailed exploration of their interactions with bacterial and cancer cell lines. Understanding these mechanisms is essential to optimize the design and synthesis of CDs containing Cu2+ ions for biomedical applications. The promising results of this study will pave the way for additional research and development of heteroatom-doped CDs with Cu2+ ion complexes, highlighting their potential as multifunctional agents in the fields of antimicrobial therapy and cancer treatment.

In this paper, we report the one-step hydrothermal synthesis of fluorine-, nitrogen-, and sulfur-doped CDs (FNS-CDs) using flufenamic acid (FFA) and thiourea (TU) as precursors, which exhibit intense yellowish-orange emission, and their application in Cu2+ ion sensing and biomedical applications (Scheme 1 ). The synthesized FNS-CDs displayed the highest excitation and emission intensities at 406 and 570 nm, respectively, with a quantum yield of 18.63 %. The surface states, morphologies, and elemental conformations of the FNS-CDs were analyzed using FTIR, XRD, TEM, and XPS. The FNS-CDs presented high sensitivity and selectivity toward Cu2+ ions with a detection limit of 83.10 nM and an association constant of 1.29 × 104 M−1 within a linear response range of 1–25 μM (R2 = 0.9972). The PL quenching of the FNS-CDs caused by Cu2+ ions was reversed by the addition of ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (EDTA). Moreover, practical applications were demonstrated by detecting Cu2+ ions in tap and lake water samples, achieving recovery rates between 97.8 % and 103.9 %, with relative standard deviation (RSD) values within 2.5 %. Additionally, the FNS-CDs exhibited substantial antibacterial activity against S. aureus (85.27 % ± 2.1 %) and E. coli (91.31 % ± 1.6 %), with enhanced effects in the presence of Cu2+ ions. The combination of FNS-CDs with Cu2+ ions (FNS-CDs/Cu2+) exhibits a higher inhibitory effect on the growth of E. coli than S. aureus. Moreover, the FNS-CDs showed more than 98 % cell viability in HCT-116 cells at concentrations up to 100 μg/mL and emitted yellowish-orange fluorescence under a confocal microscope, indicating their potential for use in bioimaging and therapeutic applications.

Flufenamic acid (FFA) and thiourea (TU) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (Seoul, South Korea). All the metal salts used in this study as nitrate and chloride salts were obtained from TCI Chemicals and Sigma Aldrich, South Korea. All the chemicals were of analytical grade and were used without further purification. Deionized water was used throughout the experiments.

The FNS-CDs were synthesized via a hydrothermal process using FFA as the fluorine and carbon source and TU as the nitrogen and sulfur source. Initially, 0.2 g of FFA and 0.1 g of TU were dissolved in ultrapure water (50 mL) and sonicated for 10 min. This solution was then transferred to an 80 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and heated at 160 °C for 8 h. After cooling to room temperature, the resulting light-yellow solution was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 30 min to remove the large precipitates. To further purify the FNS-CDs, the solution was dialyzed for 24 h in a dialysis bag with a molecular weight cutoff of 1,000. The final transparent solution of the FNS-CDs was stored at 4 °C, with a concentration of approximately 0.5 mg/mL, determined by weighing the dried FNS-CDs.

The PL spectra of the chemosensor FNS-CDs (0.5 mg/mL) in the presence of various metal ions (Ag+, Al3+, Ba2+, Ca2+, Cd2+, Ce3+, Cu2+, Fe3+, Hg2+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Na+, Ni2+, Pb2+, Sn2+, and Zn2+ ions; 25 μM) were verified in a phosphate-buffered saline (PBS) buffer of pH 7.4. For Cu2+ ion titration, chemosensor FNS-CDs (0.5 mg/mL) were dissolved in a PBS buffer of pH 7.4. Afterward, 25 μL of the chemosensor FNS-CDs and 25 μL of the Cu2+ ions were diluted to 2.950 mL in a PBS buffer of pH 7.4 to obtain the final concentration of 25 μM. A reversibility study was conducted by recording the fluorescence spectra of a solution containing FNS-CDs (0.5 mg/mL) and FNS-CDs/Cu2+ (25 μM). Subsequently, an EDTA (100 μM) solution, at the same concentration as the Cu2+ ions, was added, and its PL spectra were recorded. The PL spectra of the FNS-CDs in the presence of various competing species under the same conditions were recorded at 570 nm (excitation at 406 nm). After mixing for a few seconds, the PL spectra were recorded at room temperature.

The antibacterial activities of the FNS-CDs and FNS-CDs/Cu2+ were evaluated against E. coli and S. aureus using the colony counting method. Initially, overnight cultures of both bacterial strains were inoculated into a fresh LB medium and incubated with FNS-CDs and FNS-CDs/Cu2+ (FNS-CDs;100 μg/mL, Cu2+; 25 μM) for 12 h. Subsequently, the bacterial suspensions were diluted 1000-fold in the LB medium. Subsequently, 100 µL of the diluted FNS-CD and FNS-CDs/Cu2+ suspensions were spread onto LB agar plates using an L-shaped spreader to ensure uniform distribution. The plates were incubated at 37 °C for 12 h. Following incubation, the bacterial colonies formed on the agar plates were counted and recorded to determine the antibacterial efficacy of the FNS-CDs and FNS-CDs/Cu2+.

To evaluate the cytotoxicity of FNS-CDs on HCT-116 cell lines, cells were seeded at a density of 5.0 × 103 cells per well in a 96-well plate and allowed to adhere overnight in 500 μL of complete Dulbecco’s modified Eagle’s medium (DMEM) containing 10 % fetal bovine serum (FBS) at 37 °C in a 5 % CO₂ atmosphere. Following incubation, the medium was replaced with 500 μL of fresh DMEM containing varying concentrations of FNS-CDs (20, 40, 60, 80, and 100 μg/mL). The cells were incubated for 24 h under the same conditions. After treatment, cytotoxicity was assessed using the WST assay, where 20 μL of 5.0 mg/mL WST solution was added to each well and incubated for 4 h. The formazan crystals formed were solubilized with 150 μL of DMSO, and the absorbance was measured at 490 nm using a microplate reader. Cell viability was calculated as a percentage relative to the untreated control cells. The experiment was conducted in triplicate to ensure accuracy, and statistical analyses were performed to determine significant differences between the treated and control groups. Prior to the assay, any extracellular FNS-CDs were removed by washing the cells twice with PBS at pH 7.4 to eliminate external influences. For cell imaging involving FNS-CDs and FNS-CDs/Cu2+ with HCT-116 cell lines, following the aforementioned procedure, the fluorescence imaging of the cells was conducted using an excitation wavelength of 400 nm for FNS-CDs (100 μg/mL) and in the presence of Cu2+ ions (25 μM) in 2 mL PBS (pH 7.4). Fluorescence images were acquired using an Olympus FV1000 confocal laser-scanning microscope.

The optical properties of the FNS-CDs were examined using their UV–vis absorption and PL spectra (Fig. 1 ). The UV–vis spectrum of FNS-CDs shows absorption bands at 267 and 395 nm (Fig. 1a). These bands correspond to the π-π* transition of the graphite sp2 domain and the n-π* transition of C=X (where X =F, O, N, S) in the doped CDs [45,46], respectively. The FNS-CDs were well dispersed, exhibiting a transparent solution under visible light and strong yellowish-orange PL under UV irradiation at 365 nm (Fig. 1a, inset). Fluorescence spectroscopy revealed that the FNS-CDs exhibited the highest excitation/emission PL intensities at 406 nm/570 nm (Fig. 1b). Fig. 1c shows that, as the excitation wavelength varied from 365 to 435 nm, the emission of the FNS-CDs also changed accordingly, indicating a dependence on the excitation wavelength [47]. This relationship suggests that doping with F, N, and S atoms influences both the surface states and sizes of the CDs, leading to a relatively uniform size distribution and consistent surface states for the FNS-CDs [48]. In Fig. 1d, the normalized spectra of the FNS-CDs show that excitation at different wavelengths resulted in shifts in the emission wavelength. The PL emission shifted from 485 to 685 nm as the excitation wavelength increased from 365 to 435 nm. This shift can be attributed to variations in the sizes of the carbon cores and surface functional groups. The presence of multiple fluorophores on the CD surface contributed to a broad range of emission wavelengths.

To assess the optical stability of FNS-CDs, various factors, including pH, ionic strength, UV light exposure over time, and storage conditions, were investigated to determine their influence on PL intensity. Fig. 2 a shows the PL intensity of the FNS-CD peaks at pH 3, with more than 50 % intensity retained at both pH 3 and 11. This behavior is likely due to the protonation/deprotonation of the functional groups on the FNS-CD surface [49]. At pH 7, which is close to physiological pH (7.4), the PL intensity remained at 95 % of its maximum value, indicating the potential suitability of FNS-CDs for biomedical applications. As shown in Fig. 2b, the effect of ionic strength on the PL intensity was evaluated by adding NaCl (0–1.0 M) to the FNS-CDs in 10 mM PBS (pH 7.4) [50]. Within this range, the ionic strength has a minimal influence on the PL of the FNS-CDs, suggesting robustness against variations in salt concentration. Photostability testing under continuous 365 nm UV light irradiation (Fig. 2c) revealed only a slight decrease (∼3%) in the PL intensity after 30 min, indicating good resistance to photobleaching. Fig. 2d illustrates the storage stability of the FNS-CDs over 60 days under ambient conditions. The PL emission intensity showed minor fluctuations with no visible precipitation, indicating excellent stability and a long shelf life. Additionally, the quantum yield of the FNS-CDs was determined to be 18.63 %, calibrated against quinine sulfate in 0.1 M H₂SO₄ as a standard, demonstrating their efficient emission capabilities. Overall, these findings underscore the robust optical stability of the FNS-CDs under various environmental conditions, reinforcing their potential for diverse applications, including biomedical and environmental sensing.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Surface and morphological properties of FNS-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The FTIR spectra provided valuable insights into the surface functional groups present on the synthesized FNS-CDs (Fig. 3 a). The analysis revealed distinct absorption peaks corresponding to specific molecular vibrations. Notably, the peaks detected at 3500 and 3000 cm−1 are attributed to O−H and N−H bonds, respectively, indicative of hydroxyl and amino groups on the FNS-CD surface [51]. The peak at 1692 cm−1 suggests the occurrence of C=O groups, whereas the peaks at 2879, 1567, 1453, 1392, 1141, and 685 cm−1 are related to C–H, C=C–N–H, C–N, O–H, C–F, and C−S bending vibrations, respectively [45,52]. Furthermore, the peak at 1017 cm−1 specifies the stretching vibration of –C–O–C, confirming the presence of ether linkages. The XRD pattern of the FNS-CDs reveals a graphitic structure characteristic diffraction peak at 2θ = 20.7° [53], attributed to the (002) hkl plane (JCPDS card no. 26–1076) (Fig. 3b). The broadening of this peak indicated the nanoscale size of the FNS-CDs, which was consistent with the high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image. The TEM images depict well-dispersed, spherical FNS-CDs without aggregation (Fig. 3c), and the inset HRTEM images exhibit apparent lattice fringes with an interplanar spacing of 0.23 nm [54], corresponding closely to the (100) facet of graphite carbon. A statistical analysis of the TEM data (Fig. 3d) indicated that the FNS-CDs had a size distribution ranging from 2 to 8 nm, with an average diameter of approximately 4.0 nm. This comprehensive characterization underscores the importance of synthesizing FNS-CDs with defined surface functional groups and nanoscale dimensions, which are crucial for their potential applications.

XPS was employed to conduct detailed surface characterization and elemental analysis of the FNS-CDs, as depicted in Fig. 4 . The XPS survey spectra (Fig. 4a) revealed distinct peaks corresponding to F1s, N1s, S2p, C1s, and O1s at the binding energies of 690, 400, 160, 285, and 530 eV, respectively. These peaks correspond to the atomic percentages of 3.17 % for fluorine, 14.36 % for nitrogen, 6.38 % for sulfur, 59.16 % for carbon, and 16.53 % for oxygen, indicating the elemental composition of the FNS-CDs. High-resolution XPS spectra provided insights into the chemical bonding and functional groups present in the FNS-CDs. The F1s spectrum (Fig. 4b) exhibited peaks at 687.3 eV, attributed to C–F bond [38], indicating fluorine incorporation. The N1s spectrum (Fig. 4c) showed peaks at 399.5 eV (C–N–C) and 401.7 eV (N–H), confirming the presence of nitrogen in the form of both carbon–nitrogen bonds and amino groups. An analysis of the S2p spectrum (Fig. 4d) revealed peaks at 162.7 and 164.1 eV, corresponding to C–S bonds (S2p1/2 and S2p3/2), suggesting sulfur incorporation primarily in its reduced form rather than oxidized states [55].

The C1s spectrum (Fig. 4e) displayed peaks at 284.9 eV (C–C/C–N), 285.7 eV (C–O), 286.8 eV (C=O), and 288.1 eV (C–F), indicating the presence of various carbon bonding environments, including aromatic carbon, carbonyl, and fluorinated carbon species. Similarly, the O1s spectrum (Fig. 4f) exhibited peaks at 530.5 eV (C–O), 531.3 eV (C=O), and 532.7 eV (O–C=O), corresponding to different oxygen functionalities, such as hydroxyl, carbonyl, and carboxyl groups. Overall, the combined analysis of the FTIR and XPS spectra confirmed the co-doping of fluorine, nitrogen, and sulfur atoms in the FNS-CD structure. Additionally, the presence of surface functional groups, such as hydroxyl, carboxyl, and amino groups, further enhances the potential applications of CDs in diverse fields, including catalysis and biomedicine.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selective and sensitive detection of Cu2+ ions
ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescent chemosensors offer several advantages, including operational simplicity, low detection limits, real-time detection capabilities, and high portability. The PL selectivity of FNS-CDs toward a series of cations, including Ag+, Al3+, Ba2+, Ca2+, Cd2+, Ce3+, Cu2+, Fe3+, Hg2+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Na+, Ni2+, Pb2+, Sn2+, and Zn2+ ions, was investigated in PBS buffer solutions (pH 7.4) using fluorescence spectroscopy. As shown in Fig. 5 a, free sensor FNS-CDs exhibited a PL emission at 570 nm (λexc 406 nm) [56]. However, they showed remarkable PL quenching with a significant red-shift in the presence of Cu2+ ions from 570 to 624 nm in an aqueous medium, and other metal ions retained only the FNS-CD emission profile [57] (Fig. 5b). As shown in Fig. 5c, an evident colorless solution was observed in FNS-CDs with the addition of Cu2+ ions in daylight, whereas the addition of Cu2+ ions to FNS-CDs induced the quenching of PL emission with a color change from yellowish-orange to colorless, indicating that the PL was quenched by Cu2+ ions owing to their paramagnetic nature [58]. Therefore, only the introduction of Cu2+ ions resulted in a remarkable PL change at 624 nm (λexc 406 nm), which indicated the high selectivity of FNS-CDs to Cu2+ ions. The PL properties of the FNS-CDs titrated with Cu2+ ions were further studied.

Upon the addition of various Cu2+ ion concentrations (1–25 μM) to a solution of FNS-CDs (Fig. 6 a), the PL intensity at 570 nm significantly quenched with a large red-shift at 624 nm shown by color changes from orange to colorless (inset Fig. 6a), gradually reaching a minimum intensity of Cu2+ ions (25 μM). Job’s method was applied for PL measurements to determine the stoichiometry of the FNS-CDs/Cu2+ complex. Fig. 6b shows a plot of the PL intensity versus the molar fraction of Cu2+ ions, which reached a maximum when the ratio was 0.5, indicating a 1:1 stoichiometry in the FNS-CDs/Cu2+ complex [59]. Fig. 6c illustrates a linear relationship (R2 = 0.9972) between P0/P and [Cu2+] concentrations across the range of 1–17 μM (upto 25 μM), where P0 and P represent the PL intensities at 570 to 624 nm in the absence and presence of Cu2+, respectively. The association constant (K) obtained from the Stern–Volmer equation was 1.29 × 104 M−1. The limit of detection for the FNS-CDs toward Cu2+ ions was calculated as 83.10 nM (Fig. 6d), underscoring the sensitivity of the FNS-CDs for quantitatively detecting Cu2+ concentrations in aqueous solutions [60]. The sensitivity of our proposed strategy is lower compared to most of the methods reported in Table S1.

The performance evaluation of the FNS-CD sensor depends significantly on its high selectivity toward Cu2+ ions as a competitive species. To assess this selectivity, the PL changes of the FNS-CDs were examined upon exposure to 25 μM Cu2+ ions alongside 100 μM of different interfering cations, including Ag+, Al3+, Ba2+, Ca2+, Cd2+, Ce3+, Fe3+, Hg2+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Na+, Ni2+, Pb2+, Sn2+, and Zn2+. The results depicted in Fig. 7 a and b indicate that the PL intensity of the FNS-CDs was effectively quenched by Cu2+ ions, even in the presence of competing metal ions. This observation validates the selective Cu2+ response of the FNS-CDs, demonstrating negligible interference from common metal ions, including Fe3+ [60], which compete strongly in sensor applications. Complementing the PL spectra, Fig. 7c illustrates the distinct PL color changes of the FNS-CDs from yellowish-orange to colorless under 365 nm UV light in the presence of Cu2+ ions. In contrast, the presence of other metal cations did not induce prominent alterations in the PL color of FNS-CDs/Cu2+, reaffirming the exceptional selectivity of the FNS-CDs for Cu2+ ions. Overall, these findings highlight the remarkable selectivity and sensitivity of the FNS-CDs as sensors for Cu2+ ions in PBS buffer solutions (pH 7.4). This capability makes the FNS-CDs promising candidates for applications requiring the precise and reliable detection of Cu2+ ions in complex sample matrices.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Reversibility and time response of FNS-CDs toward Cu2+
ce:section.ce:para: The reversibility and regeneration capabilities of PL chemosensors are crucial for practical applications, ensuring their usability over multiple cycles. The interaction of the FNS-CDs with Cu2+ ions was observed to be reversible, as demonstrated by experiments using EDTA, a known chelating agent for metal ions. Initially, the addition of EDTA (25 μM) to a solution containing FNS-CDs (0.5 mg/mL) and Cu2+ ions (25 μM) in pH 7.4 PBS buffer showed a noticeable improvement in the PL intensity [61,62] (Fig. 8 a). This enhancement in the PL intensity indicated the release of Cu2+ ions from the FNS-CDs, effectively regenerating free FNS-CDs. Concurrently, the PL color of the FNS-CDs/Cu2+ complex shifted from colorless to yellowish-orange upon the addition of EDTA (Fig. 8b), further confirming the restoration of the initial state of the FNS-CDs.

To assess the reproducibility of this reversibility, the FNS-CDs were subjected to seven cycles of alternating addition of Cu2+ ions and EDTA (Fig. 8c). After each addition of Cu2+, the PL signal reverted to yellowish-orange, demonstrating that more than 90 % of the original PL intensity was recovered consistently over multiple cycles [63]. This robust reversibility underscores the reliability and practical utility of FNS-CDs as reversible chemosensors. Furthermore, the response time of the FNS-CDs to Cu2+ ions was evaluated to characterize their sensing performance (Fig. 8d). The results showed that the FNS-CDs rapidly interacted with Cu2+ ions within the first 4 min, with minimal fluorescence changes observed beyond 40 min. This rapid response highlights the high reactivity of the FNS-CDs toward Cu2+ ions [64], making them suitable for real-time monitoring applications in environmental analysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of FNS-CDs for Cu2+ in real sample analysis
ce:section.ce:para: To assess the practical applicability of FNS-CDs in real-world scenarios, their efficiency in detecting Cu2+ ions in tap water and lake water was evaluated using a PL method. Artificially contaminated samples were prepared by spiking known concentrations of Cu2+ ions (5, 15, and 25 μM), and these samples were analyzed using the proposed sensing method. Each measurement was repeated thrice to verify the precision and dependability of the outcomes. The experimental data yielded recovery values ranging from 97.8 % to 103.9 %, indicating that the measured concentrations closely matched the expected values after accounting for the sample preparation and analysis processes. This high level of recovery suggests that the FNS-CDs effectively detected and quantified Cu2+ ions in real samples with minimal deviation (Table 1 ). In addition, the RSD values were within 2.5 % for all measurements. The low RSD values further underscore the precision and consistency of the FNS-CDs as sensors for quantifying trace levels of Cu2+ ions in complex matrices [65], such as tap water and lake water. These results confirm the practical applicability and reliability of the PL method using FNS-CDs for the detection of Cu2+ ions in environmental samples. The ability of this method to achieve accurate measurements with high recovery rates and low variability makes it suitable for the routine monitoring and assessment of Cu2+ contamination in water sources, highlighting its potential for broader environmental and analytical applications.

Ultrasmall FNS-CDs, characterized by their highly positive charge, exhibit notable antibacterial properties, which are believed to originate from interactions with bacterial membranes that disrupt their integrity. To validate this hypothesis, the antimicrobial activities of both FNS-CDs and the FNS-CDs/Cu2+ complex were comprehensively evaluated under dark and light conditions, with control experiments conducted under identical settings (Fig. 9 ). The results shown in Fig. 9 demonstrate that both FNS-CDs and the FNS-CDs/Cu2+ complex exerted substantial antibacterial effects against S. aureus and E. coli in the absence of any treatment [66,67]. At a higher concentration (100 μg/mL), the FNS-CDs inhibited the growth of E. coli by 45.1 ± 2.3 % and S. aureus by 39.57 ± 1.9 %. Meanwhile, the FNS-CDs/Cu2+ complex (FNS-CDs;100 μg/mL, Cu2+; 25 μM) exhibited enhanced antibacterial activity, inhibiting E. coli growth by 91.31 ± 1.6 % and S. aureus growth by 85.27 ± 2.1 %.

Notably, the antibacterial effect of the FNS-CDs/Cu2+ complex on E. coli exceeded 90 %, indicating potent bactericidal properties [68,69]. Further analysis indicated that the slower release mechanism enabled the FNS-CDs/Cu2+ complex to effectively inhibit S. aureus growth by > 75 %. These observations highlight the superior efficacy of the complex against E. coli compared with S. aureus under the tested conditions. The antibacterial mechanism of both the FNS-CDs and the FNS-CDs/Cu2+ complex is attributed to their ability to generate ROS on their surfaces. These ROS molecules deactivate bacterial cells by oxidizing their membranes and disrupting their structural integrity [70]. Therefore, the broad-spectrum antibacterial activities of the FNS-CDs and FNS-CDs/Cu2+ complex observed at FNS-CDs (100 μg/mL) and Cu2+ (25 μM) underscore their potential for combating bacterial infections. These findings contribute to the understanding of their antibacterial mechanisms and highlight their promising applications in medical and environmental fields that require effective antimicrobial agents.

To further assess the practical application of the chemosensor in biological contexts, cytotoxicity tests were conducted using HCT-116 cells treated with varying concentrations of FNS-CDs, ranging from 0 to 100 μg/mL, as determined by the WST assay (Fig. 10 a). The results indicated that more than 98 % of the cells were viable at all tested concentrations of the FNS-CDs, demonstrating their non-cytotoxic nature under the experimental conditions employed [57,71]. Moreover, the potential utility of the FNS-CDs and FNS-CDs/Cu2+ complex in live HCT-116 cells was investigated using fluorescence microscopy. After incubation with the FNS-CDs (0.5 mg/mL) for 30 min at 37 °C, strong yellowish-orange fluorescence was observed within the intracellular regions of the cells [72] (Fig. 10b). Subsequent addition of 25 μM CuCl2 to the growth medium for another 30 min led to a noticeable decrease in intracellular fluorescence, indicating the ability of the chemosensor to detect and respond to Cu2+ ions within live cells. Further analysis with fluorescence and bright-field overlay images confirmed that the fluorescence signals were localized predominantly within the intracellular compartments, suggesting effective cell membrane permeability [57,72,73] of both the FNS-CDs and FNS-CDs/Cu2+ complex (Fig. 10b). This capability underscores their potential use in Cu2+ imaging applications in living cells and in vivo settings. These results highlight the dual capabilities of FNS-CDs as noncytotoxic probes and efficient intracellular Cu2+ sensors. These findings support the potential of FNS-CDs for bioimaging applications and offer insights into their practical use in biological and biomedical research aimed at visualizing and monitoring metal ion dynamics within cellular environments.

In conclusion, the Fluorine, Nitrogen, and Sulfur doped photoluminescent carbon dots (FNS-CDs) synthesized via a one-step hydrothermal method exhibited excellent properties for detecting Cu2+ ions and potential biomedical applications. The UV–vis spectra of the FNS-CDs demonstrated the n-π* transition at 269 nm and π-π* transition at 403 nm. The FNS-CDs exhibited a significant PL response with an excitation/emission maximum at 406/570 nm. Additionally, the FNS-CDs exhibited remarkable stability in various pH environments and ionic strengths, and under prolonged UV irradiation. The structural, morphological, and elemental details of the synthesized FNS-CDs were validated using FTIR, XRD, TEM, and XPS. The XRD pattern of the FNS-CDs showed a broad diffraction peak at 2θ = 20.7°, indicating a typical graphitic structure. The TEM images demonstrated that the FNS-CDs are well-dispersed and spherical, with no aggregation and an average particle size of approximately 4.0 nm. The HRTEM images showed well-resolved lattice fringes with an interplanar spacing of 0.23 nm. The FTIR and XPS results confirmed the perfect co-doping of F, N, and S atoms in the CDs. Additionally, various related functional groups, such as –OH, –COOH, and –NH2 groups, were present on the surface of the FNS-CDs. A highly selective and sensitive PL chemosensor for Cu2+ ions was reported using the FNS-CDs as probe molecules in PBS solutions at pH 7.4. Upon the addition of Cu2+, the PL intensity at 570 nm was quenched, accompanied by a red shift to 624 nm, indicating a strong and selective interaction with Cu2+. The sensor showed a linear PL response to Cu2+ ion concentrations ranging from 1 to 25 μM, with a detection limit of 83.10 nM and an association constant of 1.29 × 104 M−1. Practical applications of the FNS-CDs were demonstrated through successful Cu2+ ion detection in real water samples, with recovery rates between 97.8 % and 103.9 % and RSD values within 2.5 %. Moreover, the FNS-CDs/Cu2+ complex inhibited the growth of E. coli (91.31 %±1.6 %) and S. aureus (85.27 %±2.1 %), whereas the FNS-CDs alone inhibited E. coli (45.1 %±2.3 %) and S. aureus (39.57 %±1.9 %). Furthermore, the HCT-116 cells exhibited more than 98 % viability at FNS-CD concentrations up to 100 μg/mL, demonstrating non-cytotoxicity, highlighting their potential in bioimaging and therapeutic applications.
